The present report contains the budget performance and the budget for the United Nations Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS) for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 respectively.

The Mission operated under a challenging environment during the 2024/25 period, with political tensions that limited progress towards the implementation of the Revitalized Agreement on the Resolution of the Conflict in the Republic of South Sudan and stalled peace implementation mechanisms. Conflicts intensified in many parts of the country, with the Mission continuing to conduct patrols to protect civilians, deter violence and enable the safe movement of United Nations and humanitarian actors. The Mission continued the monitoring and investigation of human rights violations, which contributed to early warning mechanisms and enhanced the protection of civilians. With regard to elections, the Mission organized capacity-building activities and enhanced awareness-raising programmes for key electoral institutions and stakeholders in preparation for elections that are now envisioned for December 2026. In addition, quick-impact projects were implemented to strengthen operational capacity and public trust in electoral institutions throughout South Sudan.

During the 2026/27 period, UNMISS will continue to advance its strategic priorities as outlined in the multi-year strategic vision (2024) to support the implementation of the Revitalized Agreement, to enhance general security for the facilitation of humanitarian assistance and creating conditions conducive for the safe and voluntary return of refugees and internally displaced persons, and to intensify grass-roots civil and political engagements to promote peaceful coexistence and mobilize communities to support the political processes in South Sudan. The Mission will support rule of law institutions, promoting accountability and access to justice, enabling self-reliance through the identification and resolution of critical gaps, institutional support and capacity-building, and enhance strategic partnerships and promote greater coherence between international partners to advance progress in the peace process. Support will be extended to key government institutions, including relevant ministries, the national parliament, the National Constitutional Review Commission, the National Elections Commission and the Political Parties Council, to promote inclusive governance and facilitate the conduct of credible, peaceful and inclusive elections. The Mission will also support the constitution-making process and foster trust among all parties to strengthen peaceful coexistence and sociopolitical cohesion throughout the transitional period.

UNMISS incurred $1,199.4 million in expenditure for the 2024/25 period, representing a resource utilization rate of 96.9 per cent, compared with $1,154.9 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 99.2 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $38.5 million reflects the net impact of: (a) increased requirements for military and police personnel attributable primarily to expenditure deferred from the 2022/23 period and charged to the 2024/25 period, as part of the Mission’s prioritization of resources to cover funding shortfalls; (b) reduced requirements for civilian personnel attributable to higher actual average vacancy rates compared with the budgeted rates due to the recruitment freeze established to address liquidity challenges; and (c) reduced requirements for operational costs attributable primarily to the non-deployment of additional military and police personnel for which provisions for operational costs were included in the approved budget, lower costs for the rental and operation of fixed-wing aircraft due to the non-utilization of the C-130 aircraft contracted for shared use across missions under a letter of assist, given the relatively high cost associated with its use, and the prioritization of resources aimed at curtailing operational costs in response to the ongoing liquidity challenges.

The proposed budget for the 2026/27 period in the amount of $1,057.6 million represents a decrease of $128.8 million, or 10.9 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $1,186.4 million approved for the 2025/26 period.

The proposed budget reflects efficiencies and cost-reduction measures resulting in reduced requirements for: (a) military and police personnel attributable to the planned deployment of fewer uniformed personnel; (b) reduced requirements for civilian personnel attributable to the proposed abolishment of posts and positions; and (c) reduced requirements for operational costs owing to lower costs resulting from the reduced deployment of uniformed and civilian personnel.

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of United Nations Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS) was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1996 (2011). The mandate for the performance period was provided in its resolutions 2729 (2024), 2778 (2025) and 2779 (2025). The most recent extension of the mandate was authorized by the Council in its resolution 2779 (2025), by which it extended the mandate until 30 April 2026.

2.	The Mission is mandated to help the Security Council to advance a multi-year strategic vision to prevent a return to civil war in South Sudan, to build durable peace at the local and national levels and to support inclusive and accountable governance and free, fair and peaceful elections in accordance with the Revitalized Agreement on the Resolution of the Conflict in the Republic of South Sudan.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

3.	During the reporting period, limited progress towards the implementation of the Revitalized Agreement was observed. The transitional period of the Revitalized Agreement was extended for the fourth time by two years, until February 2027. However, inclusive peace implementation mechanisms stalled in 2025 owing to political and security tensions between the principal parties to the Revitalized Agreement.

4.	Beginning in February 2025, the unilateral dismissal of opposition officials from the Revitalized Transitional Government of National Unity intensified political volatility and increased tensions among the signatories of the Revitalized Agreement. Political tensions escalated further in March 2025 with the arrest of the First Vice-President, the leader of the main opposition party, the Sudan People’s Liberation Movement-Army in Opposition, following the Nasir incident. The erosion of trust between the principal parties continued to fuel insecurity.

5.	In parallel to the heightened political tensions, the conflict between the South Sudan People’s Defence Forces and opposition forces intensified significantly and spread in many parts of the country. The expansion of the conflict during the period, including the targeting of opposition cantonment and training sites established under the auspices of the Revitalized Agreement, as well as the indiscriminate use of barrel bombs in several States across the country, continued to pose serious concerns to the protection of civilians mandate of the Mission.

6.	Within this context, the whole-of-mission approach to the protection of civilians mandate continued through key leadership engagements at the national and subnational levels. Those efforts were intended to promote the cessation of hostilities, encourage a return to dialogue and advocate for the release of all detained political leaders. They also enabled the creation of a protective presence and facilitated activities designed to address key conflict drivers. Dialogues facilitated at the subnational level supported addressing land disputes, engaging with holdout groups and promoting peaceful coexistence among host communities and cattle herders, while also addressing cross-border tensions and ethnopolitical dynamics. Support was extended to State and local authorities in the development and implementation of the framework and area-based strategies and plans related to returns, reintegration and recovery. The Mission continued its efforts to prevent sexual and gender-based violence, combat conflict-related sexual violence and end violence against children. In addition, young people and women were trained on human rights standards, safe reporting and referral mechanisms, and constructive engagement with state authorities to strengthen rights protection, accountability and community safety.

7.	A total of 36,990 patrols were conducted to protect civilians, deter all forms of violence and enable the safe movement of the United Nations and humanitarian actors throughout South Sudan. Patrols included short- and long-duration patrols, dynamic air and riverine patrols and targeted patrols to combat conflict-related sexual violence. More than 6,000 members of the South Sudan National Police Service and law enforcement agencies were trained on basic policing capacities. Such capacities included public order management and crime scene investigation, enforced protection of civilians and better response to sexual and gender-based violence while upholding international standards. The Government created sexual and gender-based violence national and subnational task forces to accurately map hotspots, strengthen accountability and support a timelier and coordinated response for victims. The Mission supported the restoration of the presence of justice actors in priority locations, facilitating the voluntary return of displaced populations.

8.	The persistent political tensions severely compromised the integrity of the Revitalized Agreement and, consequently, diminished the confidence of the population, peace partners and international community in the country’s ability to conclude its prolonged transitional phase through peaceful and credible elections. The Tumaini Initiative, supported by the Government of Kenya, between the Revitalized Transitional Government and non-signatory groups did not yield the anticipated results and has remained suspended since late 2024. These developments have significantly undermined the letter and the spirit of the Revitalized Agreement and associated initiatives to expand inclusivity of the peace process, which affected mandate implementation.

9.	Within this context, the UNMISS leadership deepened the exercise of its good offices role and regularly engaged the leadership of the parties to the Revitalized Agreement at the national and subnational levels to de-escalate conflict and encourage dialogue. The Mission continued to strengthen its political engagements through the trilateral mechanism comprising the African Union, the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD) and the United Nations to urge dialogue and inclusive return to the Revitalized Agreement.

10.	In parallel, UNMISS and the trilateral mechanism engaged with political parties, the Revitalized Transitional Government, technical institutions related to electoral and constitution-making processes, civil society and regional and international stakeholders. The support of UNMISS for the permanent constitution-making process also continued, including facilitating the planning and resource mobilization efforts of the National Constitutional Review Commission to enable the launch of its civic education and consultation campaign. At the end of the reporting period, the Commission remained underresourced in terms of being able to carry out mandated tasks. In the same vein, UNMISS also supported the Revitalized Transitional Government in organizing high-level forums on fiscal federalism and completing the political transition.

11.	The Mission made progress in establishing a foundation for national and subnational dialogues among stakeholders, including political parties, the Revitalized Transitional Government and communities, helping to break down barriers to discussing sensitive topics in public forums. National-level dialogue platforms facilitated discussions among political parties on key political issues, including the path forward for the political transition. Youth representatives from various political parties developed an action plan to strengthen youth political participation in South Sudan and established a committee, “Concerned Youth Leaders South Sudan”, independently committing to refining and implementing the plan through stakeholder engagement. More than 260 women participated in a high-level conference organized with UNMISS support aimed at promoting the role of women leaders in peacefully ending the transitional period and beyond. The conference resulted in a communiqué in which the importance of full, equal and meaningful participation of women in all political processes was reaffirmed.

12.	The Mission continued to undertake capacity-building and awareness-raising programmes, fostering an enabling environment for elections and broadening civic and political participation. The Mission carried out multifaceted capacity-building initiatives for institutions involved in electoral processes and preparations and supported civic education outreach and stakeholder engagement. The Mission also assisted in operationalizing electoral institutions at the state levels. In collaboration with the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), the Mission supported the National Elections Commission in operationalizing State High Election Committees by ensuring functional workspaces for electoral officials and enhancing their capacity to manage inclusive and credible electoral processes. Through quick-impact projects, the Mission supported the operationalization of the Committees across all 10 states. The Mission trained more than 300 journalists and human rights defenders on electoral analysis and reporting, the electoral legal framework, freedom of expression and countering hate speech, thereby enhancing their knowledge of the electoral process and the role of the media. The Mission and UNDP supported the South Sudan National Police Service in conducting needs assessment visits to all 10 states in preparation for establishing joint election operations centres at both the national and state levels, which will serve as hubs for planning, coordination and collaboration on election security.

13.	The National Elections Commission and the Political Parties Council were supported in developing and adopting regulatory and policy frameworks for peaceful, transparent and inclusive electoral processes, as well as the efficient and effective execution of their mandated tasks. Capacity-building activities were organized for key electoral institutions and stakeholders, including security actors, the judiciary, line ministries, academia and civil society organizations, including those focused on the inclusion of women and young people. The activities enhanced stakeholders’ understanding of election planning and implementation, legal frameworks and timelines, the inclusion of marginalized groups, and strategies for mitigating electoral violence. The Mission supported the National Elections Commission in developing and adopting its civic and voter education strategy, as well as civic education manuals and outreach initiatives aimed at building an informed society and promoting an inclusive electoral process. The Mission also supported the Commission in engaging various stakeholders, including political parties, government institutions, civil society organizations, faith-based groups and the media, on various aspects of the electoral process at both the national and subnational levels.

14.	The last meeting of the joint task force on elections and constitution-making was held in July 2023 following its establishment a couple of months earlier. Consequently, the Revitalized Transitional Government and the signatories to the Revitalized Agreement have made limited progress on key prerequisites for holding elections and advancing the constitution-making process, in particular in areas such as electoral security and voter registration. Meanwhile, the trilateral mechanism, composed of the African Union, IGAD and the United Nations, has continued to advocate for inclusive decision-making by the Revitalized Transitional Government regarding the constitution-making and electoral processes.

15.	The Mission continued to monitor and investigate violations of human rights and international humanitarian law, thereby contributing to early warning, decision-making and technical support to national and local initiatives, ultimately enhancing the protection of civilians and promoting accountability.

16.	The Government advanced the transitional justice process in South Sudan by initiating the selection process of the commissioners for the Commission for Truth, Reconciliation and Healing, comprising four South Sudanese nationals and three non-South Sudanese. In line with the request from the Minister of Justice and Constitutional Affairs, the United Nations and the African Union Commission established a joint technical working group to coordinate the selection of the three non-South Sudanese commissioners. This joint coordination underscores the commitment to a transparent and inclusive process, leveraging the expertise and experience of the United Nations and the Commission to identify suitable candidates.

17.	Progress was made in strengthening the Comprehensive Action Plan to End and Prevent All Grave Violations against Children. UNMISS provided technical expertise, strategic advice and logistical support to technical-level committees, supporting joint verification, screening and awareness-raising field missions. The support was complemented by targeted capacity-building and awareness-raising activities. As a result, armed forces and groups vacated schools and hospitals and children were identified and released from their ranks, contributing to an enhanced commitment to child protection in South Sudan. A national conference on preventing the recruitment and use of children brought together diverse stakeholders, including the Vice-President and the Chair of the Gender and Youth Cluster, as well as representatives from high-level interministerial and technical-level committees. The conference led to the signing of military command orders to end and prevent grave violations against children and a pledge to establish a child protection desk in the office of presidency.

18.	The Mission supported the drafting, validation and launch of the five-year national strategy for the judiciary. More than 50 local and international organizations launched an open letter campaign urging the lawmakers to pass the first land policy in South Sudan without delay. The policy would address the issues of land-grabbing and land tenure, highlighting its importance for peace, equity and sustainable development. Mobile courts were deployed to enhance access to justice in underserved areas, including areas of anticipated return for internally displaced persons. On-the-job mentoring and capacity enhancement were provided during the deployment of 19 mobile military and civilian courts in key hotspots, reaching underserved areas and remote locations in nine states. The Mission supported the rehabilitation of infrastructure in the states to facilitate the deployment of the nominated judges to jurisdictions that had no judicial officers, thereby promoting access to justice in a coordinated and effective manner.

19.	The number of people in need of humanitarian support increased from 9 million to 9.3 million. More than 700,000 new internally displaced persons were registered in South Sudan and more than 500,000 people entered the country from the Sudan. In response, the Mission supported efforts to improve humanitarian access and create the conditions conducive for the delivery of humanitarian assistance through engagements in humanitarian coordination forums and protection cluster meetings. The engagements facilitated access, enhanced early warning and response mechanisms to address threats and strengthened situational awareness and coordinated action for emerging humanitarian situations.

20.	Mine action activities provided a secure environment for communities, promoting the safe return and reintegration of internally displaced persons and refugees. Initiatives targeted community members and mission and humanitarian personnel, enabled the mobility of mission staff and goods/cargo and allowed for the delivery of humanitarian aid across the country. Technical assistance and advice to the Government of South Sudan was provided in capacity-building activities and in the development of the national mine action gender equality and diversity policy.

21.	UNMISS and the United Nations country team collaborated on awareness-raising and response to the effects of climate change, including flooding across the country. The country’s national flood preparedness and response strategy (2024–2025) was anchored in forward-looking, scenario-based planning designed to enhance anticipatory action and coordinated risk mitigation. A cornerstone of that approach was the activation of the “Information Management Working Group Plus”, an expanded analytical platform comprising UNMISS, United Nations agencies, funds and programmes, development partners and national authorities. The Working Group conducted a rigorous multi-agency analysis, resulting in three plausible flood scenarios that guided preparedness and operational planning at the national and subnational levels.

22.	System-wide coordination and integrated planning and programming for enhanced coherence between the Mission and the United Nations agencies, funds and programmes supported complementarity between mandated activities and implementation of the United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Framework for South Sudan (2023–2025). The Mission led joint electoral support for the Revitalized Transitional Government through the integrated electoral assistance team with UNDP and the broader United Nations country team. The Mission worked with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes on area-based projects funded by the United Nations multi-partner trust fund (specifically, the South Sudan reconciliation, stabilization and resilience trust fund), with a focus on reconciliation and community engagement efforts, community policing and trust-building between the South Sudan National Police Service and the population, thereby contributing to stabilizing efforts.

23.	The Mission implemented 62 quick-impact projects across all 10 states of South Sudan. Ten of the projects supported the operationalization of State High Elections Committee offices, one in each state. The remaining 52 projects focused on: (a) the strengthening of rule of law and the justice chain through the construction of police posts, prisons, courthouses, accommodation for judges, and prosecutors’ offices and accommodations; (b) the construction of schools, primary healthcare centres, boreholes and water yards, maternity wards, special protection units/centres and community outreach centres; and (c) the construction of offices for the human rights commission in Wau, Bentiu and Rumbek.
Mission support initiatives

24.	During the 2024/25 period, the Mission encountered significant obstacles in the area of mission support, which adversely affected the implementation of its mandate. The main constraints included persistent liquidity challenges, an unstable security environment that limited movement across the country and a directive from the Government of South Sudan to vacate the Mission’s primary logistical hub in Tomping. In response to the liquidity challenges, several measures to curtail expenditure were implemented, including a civilian hiring freeze and the reduced allotment for operational costs. With regard to the host Government’s request to vacate Tomping, the Mission began to relocate accommodation facilities to United Nations House and transferred a parcel of land to the South Sudan civil aviation authority. Those obstacles notwithstanding, the Mission continued to provide essential logistical and administrative support. UNMISS maintained effective and efficient services, strengthened internal controls and upheld strong principles of oversight and accountability.

25.	The Mission implemented key infrastructure projects to enhance the well-being, safety and security of personnel and peacekeepers, as well as to increase its capacity to implement the protection of civilians mandate through operational presence. UNMISS prioritized multi-year construction projects, which included building concrete bunkers and rehabilitating ammunition storage areas. The Mission also established four new temporary operating bases in conflict-sensitive regions, namely, Moppair, Tambura, Abiemnom and Koch, which bolstered its operational footprint. In addition, the Mission completed extensive refurbishment works at Juba headquarters and several field offices, improving the safety, living conditions and overall operational efficiency for personnel.

26.	The Mission also continued to achieve significant strides in environmental efforts. In that regard, the Mission installed 15 new waste yards, added 4 energy-efficient biofilters, 1 each in Aweil, Torit, Rumbek and Tambura, and expanded its collaboration with the Juba Electricity Distribution Company to supply Tomping and United Nations House with renewable energy. At the end of the reporting period, the work to connect the Tomping camp to the Company’s electrical grid was ongoing. To further improve energy efficiency, the Mission consolidated generators into 11 centralized powerhouses and connected 17 contingents to United Nations-owned generators, which helped to reduce fuel consumption and emissions.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27

27.	South Sudan continues to be plagued by deep political, security, humanitarian and economic challenges. Persistent tensions are likely to continue among signatories to the Revitalized Agreement, including armed conflict across the country, with grave consequences for the civilian population. Political contestation and the fragmentation of opposition groups continue to have a negative impact on the predictable implementation of the peace agreement, thereby undermining public trust and confidence in the peace process and governmental institutions.

28.	Persistent intercommunal violence, uneven implementation of the peace agreement, including slow progress to exit the transitional period with peaceful and credible elections, and fiscal austerity measures continue to shape the Mission’s operating environment, requiring a flexible, threat-based approach and cost-sensitive planning. The Mission will continue to prioritize a three-tiered approach in its protection of civilians mandate, encompassing dialogue and engagement, the provision of physical protection by maintaining a flexible and robust posture to deter violence in risk-prone areas, and fostering a protective environment by addressing root causes of protection risks. Early warning mechanisms will be strengthened to identify conflict areas and guide the strategic deployment of forces through integrated patrols, to prevent violence against civilians and to foster a protective environment.

29.	The Mission will, in coordination with the United Nations country team and the humanitarian country team, promote coherent long-term approaches to durable solutions for internally displaced persons and address underlying drivers of threats to civilians.

30.	Social cohesion, reconciliation and community engagement remain crucial to reduce ethnic polarization, support the national peace process and repair the social fabric enhancing peaceful coexistence. Mitigation measures will be strengthened to reduce violence and communal conflicts through support for inter-party dialogues, community initiatives for reconciliation, pastoral migration management by state and local authorities, peace education and communal interdependencies. Reinforcing stakeholders’ capabilities will be targeted for tangible results at the community level and may raise the Government’s understanding of their roles and responsibilities in the peace process.

31.	Engagements with the Government will continue through capacity enhancement activities and institutional reform programmes, to build on the progress made in previous years and foster government and community ownership and responsibility for the protection of civilians. Advocacy and good offices efforts will be continued to promote consensus and a singular message from the international community towards opening civic and political space.

32.	Technical assistance to the South Sudan National Police Service and security institutions will strengthen their capacities in public order management, human rights violations investigations, accountability mechanisms, gender-responsive policy and electoral security. Activities will address community security challenges and include anti-cattle-raiding initiatives aimed at reducing intercommunal violence and strengthening social cohesion.

33.	Multiple extensions of the transitional period of the Revitalized Agreement demonstrate a lack of political will and degraded trust among the leadership of the parties to the Agreement. The political tension at the national level is correlated with increasing violence at the subnational level. In response, engagements with national stakeholders, civil society and regional actors will be pursued to foster dialogue and consensus-building on the cessation of hostilities and the full and inclusive implementation of the Revitalized Agreement to reinforce inclusive governance and reduce the likelihood of a return to violence.

34.	Targeted support for accountable government institutions, including ministries, the national parliament, the National Elections Commission, the National Constitutional Review Commission, the Political Parties Council and others, as appropriate, will be prioritized. The Mission will also continue to support platforms for inclusive and constructive political dialogue among political actors and other stakeholders and promote the opening of civic and political space. Support for critical nation-building processes and related institutions, such as the constitution-making and electoral processes, will promote inclusive and accountable governance and peaceful coexistence.

35.	In anticipation of the elections scheduled for December 2026, which would be South Sudan’s first as an independent nation, the civic and political landscape is expected to remain highly contested, given the prevailing political and security context. Incidents of electoral violence, restrictions on freedom of expression, assembly and association, and the intimidation of political opponents, civil society organizations, journalists and media outlets are expected to increase. In this context, coordinated electoral assistance, including good offices, will be prioritized by UNMISS and the broader international community, as well as donor partners, to expand civic and political space, in close collaboration with national electoral stakeholders. Stakeholder engagement, civic education and outreach initiatives at the field offices level will be prioritized to support the State High Election Committees and other key actors. These efforts will be complemented by raising awareness of the electoral process, including the importance of inclusive and credible elections for the country’s peaceful conclusion of the transitional period of the Revitalized Agreement. Enhanced technical support will be provided to diverse electoral stakeholders, with a focus on promoting the inclusive participation of civil society, women and youth groups, as well as other marginalized groups, thereby ensuring their electoral readiness.

36.	The National Elections Commission, the Political Parties Council, the National Constitutional Review Commission, the South Sudan National Police Service, the Revitalized Transitional National Legislative Assembly, the judiciary, the State High Election Committees and other institutions will receive targeted assistance on election security and violence prevention, in particular on the prevention of violence against women during elections. These efforts will be carried out in coordination with the United Nations country team and regional and international partners to ensure the effective integration and delivery of electoral assistance to South Sudan.

37.	The Mission’s support for the electoral process in South Sudan will continue to follow a phased approach, with the proposed resource requirements for the 2026/27 period focused on phase one activities aimed at creating conditions conducive to credible elections. These activities include strengthening civic and political space, establishing mechanisms to enhance election security, promoting inclusive civic engagement and dialogue and providing targeted technical assistance and capacity-building to national stakeholders. The scale and timing of United Nations electoral support are aligned with Security Council mandates and electoral needs assessment recommendations and remain dependent on progress in key benchmarks, including the deployment of the Necessary Unified Forces, the adoption of a permanent constitutional framework, adequate government resourcing of electoral institutions such as the National Elections Commission and the Political Parties Council, and the establishment of a clear electoral framework. The Mission will monitor political and security developments closely. Any request for additional resources for phase two to support electoral operations will depend on the progress made by the South Sudanese authorities in establishing conditions for credible, peaceful and inclusive elections, as well as on the concurrence of the Security Council.

38.	For the 2024/25 period, the Mission incurred expenditure in the amount of $6.8 million for electoral support, compared with the approved budget of $12.3 million. This reflects reduced requirements for civilian personnel and operational costs owing to austerity measures established to address the liquidity constraints, including the recruitment freeze that led to higher vacancy rates for civilian personnel. The proposed budget for the 2026/27 period includes $7.8 million for electoral support, reflecting reduced requirements for civilian personnel owing to the proposed abolishment of six positions and the application of higher vacancy rates, as well as reduced requirements for operational costs. 39.	Ongoing conflicts in neighbouring countries, in particular in the Sudan, have exacerbated the humanitarian crisis in South Sudan and adversely affected the delivery of the Mission’s mandate. Engagements with regional and international stakeholders, including the troika (Norway, the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland and the United States of America), and interested Member States will help to build a coalition of partners with one voice to provide coherent support for the political transition in South Sudan. UNMISS will continue to participate in the African Union-IGAD-United Nations trilateral mechanism and regular diplomatic discussion forums.

40.	The human rights situation in South Sudan is expected to deteriorate, driven by weak accountability mechanisms, insecurity and denials of access that prevent the Mission from reaching specific hotspots, leading to an increase in violations committed by all parties to the conflict. The Mission will continue to implement the United Nations human rights due diligence policy to ensure that all United Nations activities and those of partners are systematically assessed for potential risks of human rights violations. This will be supported through technical assistance and capacity-building initiatives aimed at enhancing all United Nations personnel’s understanding of risk assessments and mitigation strategies under the policy. Monitoring, documenting, investigating and reporting on human rights in South Sudan will remain a strategic priority, with a focus on protecting and promoting human rights, as well as addressing, preventing and reducing human rights violations, including incidents of hate speech and incitement of violence.

41.	Progress in the judiciary is expected to continue to yield opportunities. This includes expanding the presence of justice actors in underserved areas, reactivating previously inactive courts and implementing measures to enhance integrity and transparency within the justice system. The Mission will prioritize the advancement of rule of law and enhancing security sector governance, with a strategic emphasis on strengthening criminal justice institutions. Support for mobile courts will be expanded, with a special focus on addressing sexual and gender-based violence, including conflict-related sexual violence. The Mission will also focus on promoting accountability for offences committed by South Sudanese defence and security forces. Support for rule of law institutions will enhance access to justice, strengthen accountability mechanisms, expand legal aid services and promote transitional justice. These efforts will help to foster a conducive environment for transparent, inclusive and genuine consultations towards transitional justice and constitution-making and electoral processes.

42.	A survivor-centred approach to conflict-related sexual violence remains essential because such violence continues to be utilized as a tool of intimidation, control and ethnic targeting. The Mission will continue to foster integrated responses, with a focus on protection, security patrols and the prevention of violence. National security forces and local actors will be supported through awareness-raising campaigns to reduce stigma and strengthen monitoring, data analysis and reporting mechanisms. The deployment of gender advisers across all field offices will advance initiatives that promote women’s empowerment, education and participation in public decision-making and leadership for peace, while supporting the implementation of the women and peace and security resolutions of the Security Council.

43.	Notwithstanding the Revitalized Transitional Government’s commitments, trends in 2025 indicate that children remain increasingly vulnerable to grave violations amid political tensions and military confrontations. The Mission will intensify advocacy efforts, political engagements and community mobilization to urge parties to the conflict to uphold the Comprehensive Action Plan to End and Prevent All Grave Violations against Children. Targeted capacity-building activities will focus on key counterparts, including government technical committees, and community leaders to strengthen support for the monitoring and reporting mechanism.

44.	The Mission will continue to work in close coordination with humanitarian partners and relevant national actors to address immediate needs for assistance, in particular in areas of heightened vulnerability, including areas experiencing high levels of displacements and returns. UNMISS will enhance integrated patrols in areas of conflict, provide force protection for the delivery of humanitarian assistance to communities in need and support freedom of movement, especially during times of increased instability. In response to the increase in violations of the status-of-force agreement in 2025, the Mission will actively engage at the high-level coordination forum and with state-level authorities to address access denials and ensure operational continuity.

45.	Mine action efforts will continue to advance the priorities for delivery of the Mission’s mandate through the verification of main supply routes, the surveying and clearing of hazardous areas near humanitarian hubs and return sites and the provision of explosive ordnance risk education and awareness. The activities are designed to ensure freedom of movement for UNMISS patrols and humanitarian partners, while reinforcing the protection of civilians. With elections scheduled for December 2026, an increase in activities and mine action support is assumed.

46.	Recurring and severe flooding, erratic drought and other climatic shocks have further exacerbated the deteriorating economic, humanitarian and security situation in South Sudan. These environmental challenges will continue to adversely affect the Mission’s operations and mandate delivery. In response, a whole-of-mission approach will be employed to facilitate timely action and ensure the strategic allocation of resources to address climate-related risks and operational constraints.
Mission support initiatives

47.	During the 2026/27 budget period, the Mission will continue to prioritize the completion of key multi-year projects initiated during previous periods. The proposed requirements for the 2026/27 period include seven projects, including the following two major ones: the continuing construction of reinforced bunkers in compliance with the safety standards of the Department of Safety and Security; and the construction of hard-walled accommodation for contingent personnel. The Mission will also focus on the construction of concrete septic tanks, a conventional sludge wastewater treatment plant in Bor and ammunition storage areas to address environmental and safety concerns. The Mission also plans to continue to address its duty of care to mission personnel through the replacement of prefabricated accommodation that have passed their useful life and the construction of hard-walled kitchens and accommodation, in line with the United Nations commitment to the well-being of its peacekeepers under Action for Peacekeeping Plus.

48.	The proposed budget for 2026/27 reflects the Mission’s revised operational posture following the invocation of the contingency plan by the Secretary-General in October 2025, in response to prevailing liquidity constraints arising from the delayed receipt of assessed contributions from Member States. The ongoing implementation of the contingency plan includes the closure of two field offices, in Aweil and Torit, and eight company and temporary operating bases, as well as the conversion of two field offices to company operating bases across South Sudan, resulting in the repatriation of uniformed personnel and the reduction in civilian personnel and in operational expenditure. Accordingly, the 2026/27 proposed budget reflects decreased requirements associated with the sustained closure and conversion of the above-mentioned locations. Compared with the deployment prior to the implementation of the contingency plan, these adjustments reflect the reduction in the number of deployed uniformed personnel by 1,547 military and police personnel and 19 government-provided personnel, as well as the abolishment of 239 civilian posts and positions and corresponding reductions in operational costs.

49.	The closure or transition of sites and field offices, the discontinuation of some programmatic activities and a reduction in the Mission’s footprint (including the reduction of patrols and aviation assets) have significantly constrained the ability of UNMISS to carry out its core mandated functions in protection, political engagements, human rights, creating conditions for humanitarian support, support for the peace process and electoral support. The implementation of contingency measures is also taking place in the increasingly challenging political and operational context of election preparations, large-scale returns and rising intercommunal tensions, as well as insecurity and volatile cross-border dynamics.

50.	UNMISS continues to operate in an increasingly complex environment, further strained by the deteriorating security situation. The Mission is undertaking a comprehensive mission-wide process to take stock of its operating environment, the impact of its contingency plan and the potential efficiencies and recalibration of its resources in order to continue mandate delivery. This includes the revision of its planning documents and adjustments to its operational posture, to reflect the reduction in field locations, capacity and resources. Strategic guidance has been developed regarding the prioritization and sequencing of mandated activities to guide mission components through this process. In addition, an integrated planning task force has been established to ensure coordinated planning for mandate implementation following the activation of the contingency plan. The work of the task force is ongoing, with a focus on prioritizing mission operations, in line with the aforementioned strategic guidance, to adapt mandate implementation to the constraints of reduced resources, footprint and capacity. This includes revising the geographical coverage of field offices and sites, taking into account the potential reversibility of contingency measures, and strengthening partnerships with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes, as well as non-governmental organizations, where present. While the Mission will strive to sustain efficiencies in the future budget periods, there may also be a need to adjust its operations, to reinstate company operating bases and/or field offices, should they be essential to mandate implementation. For example, following the implementation of the contingency plan, the Mission’s military component has a minimal capacity to conduct monthly long-distance patrols in and around the 10 former bases and sites. The proposed return of 3,184 military contingent personnel, of the 4,509 previously repatriated under the contingency plan, would ensure that the Mission can enhance the conduct of long-distance patrols that it deems to be required, while making arrangements to sustain efficiencies achieved through the troop reductions. Throughout the implementation of the contingency plan, the Mission will seek to enhance effectiveness through organizational optimization and functional streamlining. Any efficiency gains realized will be reported in the relevant performance report.
Civilian staffing review

51.	During the 2025/26 period, the Mission conducted a civilian staffing review, in accordance with paragraph 28 of General Assembly resolution 76/274. The review was intended to align the Mission’s structure and staffing with its mandate and strategic priorities, taking into account its evolving life cycle and identifying opportunities for the nationalization of posts. The review assessed workforce capabilities, upskilling and reskilling needs, gender and diversity considerations, and the role of affiliated personnel. Given that the review was guided strongly by the maximizing of efficiencies, its recommendations highlighted opportunities to repurpose resources through anticipated vacancies, shared services, regionalization and other efficiency measures. Several recommendations have been included in the proposed budget for the 2026/27 period. The remaining recommendations will be implemented in a phased manner on the basis of the Mission’s priorities and operational imperatives.
D.	Regional mission cooperation

52.	During the 2024/25 period, the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda, continued to provide support to the Mission in the areas of human resources (onboarding and separation, benefits and payroll, including education grants and reimbursement for mission-related travel); finance (vendor payments, cashier services and financial reporting); training and conference services; logistics (transport and movement control, freight forwarding, level III medical services, printing and ground airport services in support of United Nations flights); and procurement. UNMISS continued coordination arrangements with other regional mission partners, such as the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei, to implement its mandated support for the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism, including through working group arrangements on logistics, security, operations and communications issues.
53.	During the 2026/27 period, the Mission will continue the same regional cooperation as in the 2024/25 period.

F.	Proposed staffing changes
Civilian staffing review – Abolishment

54.	The civilian staffing review identified opportunities for efficiencies through workforce optimization, as described in paragraph 51 above. Consequently, it is proposed that 17 posts and positions be abolished, as follows: 1 Principal Coordination Officer (D-1) in the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General, 3 Civil Affairs Officer (international United Nations Volunteer) in the Civil Affairs Division, 7 Human Rights Officer (1 P-4, 2 P-3 and 4 international United Nations Volunteer) in the Human Rights Division, 1 Administrative Assistant (international United Nations Volunteer) in the Transport Section and 2 Security Coordination Officer (P-3), 1 Close Protection Coordination Officer (P-3) and 2 Security Officer (Field Service) in the Safety and Security Section.
Conversions

55.	In line with the request from the General Assembly in its resolution 76/274 for further nationalization and taking into account both the capacity of national staff and the potential for increased efficiencies, the civilian staffing review recommended six nationalizations through the conversion of posts and positions. In line with that recommendation, the proposed conversions are as follows: (a) three Civil Affairs Officer positions from international United Nations Volunteer to National Professional Officer posts in the Civil Affairs Division; (b) one post of Administrative Assistant from Field Service to national General Service in the Rule of Law and Security Institutions Section; (c) one position of Supply Chain Assistant from international United Nations Volunteer to a national General Service post as Movement Control Assistant in the Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management Service; and (d) one post of Human Resources Assistant from Field Service to national General Service in the Human Resources Management Section.
Reassignments

56.	The civilian staffing review recommended reassignments to strengthen the Office of the Chief of Staff, the Conduct and Discipline Team, the Property and Acquisition Management Section, the Distribution (Movement Control) Section and the Engineering Section, as follows:
Office of the Chief of Staff

57.	The Office of the Chief of Staff lacks sufficient capacity to meet the growing demand for the data‑driven planning, reporting and policy analysis required to support decision‑making in an increasingly complex political environment. The civilian staffing review recommended repurposing an existing post to address that gap. Accordingly, it is proposed that one post of Political Affairs Officer (P-3) be reassigned from the Heads of Field Offices Section to the Office of the Chief of Staff as Data Specialist (P-3), to strengthen the Chief of Staff’s capacity in data management. The incumbent will be responsible for developing a mission‑wide data strategy, supporting the centralization of data management and ensuring effective coordination of data collection and analysis. The incumbent will also leverage the existing capabilities of the Joint Mission Analysis Centre and the Joint Operations Centre to enhance integrated analytical support across the Mission.
Conduct and Discipline Team

58.	The Conduct and Discipline Team was established in 2011, when the Mission had an authorized deployment of 11,531 civilian and uniformed personnel. For the 2026/27 period, the Mission will have an authorized deployment of 21,759 uniformed and civilian personnel, which reflects an increase of 88.7 per cent from the 2011/12 period. Accordingly, the scale and scope of the Team with respect to the implementation of its mandate, including the number of cases handled by the Team, has increased since 2011. To strengthen the Team, it is proposed that one post of Senior Electoral Officer (P-5) be reassigned from the Political Affairs Division to Senior Conduct and Discipline Officer (P-5) in the Team. The functions of the Senior Electoral Officer in the Political Affairs Division will be redistributed and absorbed by the Electoral Affairs Division.
Property and Acquisition Management Section

59.	In the Property and Acquisition Management Section, the civilian staffing review identified gaps in specialized personnel needed to collect, clean, analyse and maintain data generated through the supply chain planning tool and the Umoja supplier relationship management platform. The review also concluded that the functional title of Supply Assistant did not accurately reflect the primary duties and responsibilities associated with the property management functions. In this regard, it is proposed that one post of Acquisition Planning Assistant (Field Service) be reassigned as Data Analysis Assistant (Field Service) and that four posts of Supply Assistant (Field Service) be reassigned as Property Management Assistant (Field Service) within the Section.
Distribution (Movement Control) Section

60.	The Distribution (Movement Control) Section is responsible for coordinating the movement of all mission passengers and cargo across 13 operational locations in Kenya, South Sudan and Uganda. To manage its workload, the Section has previously relied on temporary personnel loans from the Warehouse and Commodity Management Section. The civilian staffing review recommended regularizing that arrangement to ensure adequate staffing, in particular given the significant increase in workload experienced during the implementation of the Mission’s contingency plan.

61.	In this regard, it is proposed that one post of Supply Assistant (Field Service) be reassigned from the Warehouse and Commodity Management Section to Movement Control Assistant (Field Service) in the Distribution (Movement Control) Section.
Engineering Section

62.	The civilian staffing review also recommended the consolidation of the Accommodation Management Unit’s functions into the Engineering Section, which is detailed in paragraph 67 below. To align the responsibilities within the newly integrated Section, it is proposed that one post of Administrative Assistant (Field Service) be reassigned to Facilities Management Assistant (Field Service).
Reclassifications
Security and Safety Section

63.	In the Safety and Security Section, the civilian staffing review noted that the scope of the responsibilities of the Security Coordination Officer (P-3) at Juba headquarters and the Security Coordinator Officer (P-3) in the Malakal field office was not aligned with current post grade level. The Security Coordination Officer in Juba reports directly to the Chief Security Officer (P-5), serves as deputy, assumes officer-in-charge responsibilities during absences and supervises other staff members at the P-3 level. In Malakal, the Security Coordination Officer (P-3) serves as the area security adviser, reports to the Head of Field Office (D-1) and holds senior-level responsibilities within the United Nations security management system, including security risk management and inter-agency coordination across the area security management team, in an increasingly high-risk and complex environment. The security situation in the Upper Nile has deteriorated significantly amid the ongoing conflict. This has expanded operational demands, increased exposure and necessitated frequent high-consequence decision-making regarding movement, access, risk acceptance and crisis/incident response. The role therefore requires capacity at the P-4 level for enhanced independent judgment, strategic risk analysis and representational authority to ensure uninterrupted operations while meeting duty-of-care obligations. Jonglei, a comparable complex duty station in South Sudan, already deploys a Security Coordination Officer at the P-4 level. In this context, it is proposed that two posts of Security Coordination Officer be reclassified from the P-3 to the P-4 level to reflect the higher level of responsibilities and align the supervisory level of the Field Security Coordination Officer from other field offices.
Redeployments
Office of the Chief of Staff

64.	The civilian staffing review recommended the transfer of the Board of Inquiry Unit from the Office of the Director of Mission Support Division to the Office of the Chief of Staff through the redeployment one post of Board of Inquiry Officer (P-3), one post of Board of Inquiry Assistant (Field Service) and one position of Board of Inquiry Assistant (international United Nations Volunteer). The transfer is intended to align the reporting line of the Unit with the 2017 standardized mission support structure for field missions.
Heads of Field Offices Section

65.	The Heads of Field Offices Section in Juba lacks adequate administrative support, resulting in gaps in routine functions such as scheduling meetings, maintaining records and processing correspondence. The Section has previously relied on temporary personnel loans from the State Administrative Officer Operations Section. The civilian staffing review recommended regularizing that arrangement to fill the gap. In this regard, it is proposed that one post of Team Assistant (national General Service) be redeployed from the State Administrative Officer Operations Section to the Heads of Field Offices Section.
Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management Service

66.	The civilian staffing review identified capacity gaps within the Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management Service, in particular in the oversight of procurement processes for aviation‑related contracts, in the support provided for the delivery of equipment and goods to mission locations across South Sudan and in the management of warehouse inventories. Accordingly, it is proposed that one position of Air Operations Assistant (Field Service) and one position of Movement Control Assistant (Field Service) be redeployed from the Aviation Section and the Distribution (Movement Control) Section, respectively, to the Office of the Chief of Supply Chain Management Service. This redeployment would strengthen coordination across the supply chain management pillar and enhance oversight of aviation‑related contractual activities.
Engineering Section and Accommodation Management Unit

67.	The civilian staffing review identified overlapping functions for camp support and maintenance services between the Engineering Section and the Accommodation Management Unit. To eliminate the overlap, it is proposed that the functions of the Unit be consolidated into the Section through the redeployment of 27 posts and positions (1 P-4, 2 P-3, 1 Field Service, 17 national General Service, 1 National Professional Officer and 5 United Nations Volunteer), in addition to the reassignment explained in paragraph 62 above. The redeployment will enable the Mission to conform to the 2017 mission support structure guidance with the intention of ensuring consistent and efficient service delivery across all mission areas.
Other cost-reduction measures

68.	Following the closure of field offices and bases as explained in paragraph 48 above, it is proposed that 239 posts and positions be abolished, as presented in table 3, which were deployed in the duty stations that have been closed and are not expected to be reopened during the 2026/27 period.
Continuation of general temporary assistance positions
Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General (Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator)
Senior Programme Management Officer/Chief of Mine Action Programme

69.	A position of Senior Programme Management Officer/Chief of Mine Action Programme (P-5), funded under general temporary assistance, was established on 1 July 2022 to direct the implementation of the mine action programme in UNMISS. In view of the continuation of the programme in the 2026/27 period, it is proposed that the position of Senior Programme Management Officer/Chief of Mine Action Programme (P-5) be retained. The incumbent will continue, among others, to implement the UNMISS mine action programme strategy, carry out strategic and operational planning, programme design and response to threats, provide advice to the Mission’s leadership and engage with the local authorities involved in mine action. The incumbent will also continue to oversee the delivery and performance of implementing partners.
Electoral Affairs Division

70.	The Security Council, in its resolution 2779 (2025), mandated UNMISS to use its good offices and provide appropriate technical assistance and advice to the Revitalized Transitional Government and relevant stakeholders, based on realistic planning assumptions and clear timelines. The Council requested the Mission to focus on technical advice on the conduct of elections, voter education to prevent and respond to electoral violence, and inclusive training and dialogue among political stakeholders, ensuring the full, equal, meaningful and safe participation of women, young people, internally displaced persons, refugees and other vulnerable groups in order to mitigate tensions.

71.	During the 2026/27 period, the Mission seeks to: (a) further support the creation of a conducive environment for elections through civic and voter education, working in partnership with civil society organizations, media, political parties and the United Nations country team; (b) provide technical assistance to electoral stakeholders in reviewing and drafting the electoral legal framework, including relevant rules, regulations and procedures; and (c) support election management bodies. The Mission will provide technical, operational and logistical support to the National Elections Commission in its efforts to establish itself as a fully functioning and trusted institution at both the national and subnational levels, capable of planning and executing necessary electoral preparations and conducting elections.

72.	In this regard, the continuation of 61 positions, comprising 53 general temporary assistance positions and 8 international United Nations Volunteers positions, as presented in table 4, is proposed.
Security and Safety Section

73.	The Security Officer positions funded under general temporary assistance remain an integral part of the Security and Safety Section. The Security Officers will continue to carry out critically essential duties, including providing guard force services, conducting security patrols, conducting security risk assessments, managing access control, emergency response and recovery, and conducting special investigations, security clearances and other security-related tasks. The security situation in South Sudan continues to be volatile, notwithstanding some progress made in the political process, and it is expected to worsen in the period leading up to the elections. Consequently, it is proposed that the eight temporary positions of Security Officer (Field Service) funded under general temporary assistance be retained. The positions will enable the Mission to retain experienced security officers to allow the Mission to continue to ensure the security and safety of mission personnel and assets.

table_content.text: 2.1	Conducive environment created for combating impunity for abuses and violations of human rights and international humanitarian law by all parties to the conflict

74.	During the 2024/25 period, the following evaluations conducted by the Office of Internal Oversight Services regarding UNMISS have guided planning for the 2026/27 period.
(a)	Evaluation of children and armed conflict: peacekeeping operations;
(b)	Evaluation of the implementation of the protection of civilians mandate in the lead-up to national elections.

75.	In response to the recommendations of the evaluation on children and armed conflict, the Mission is exploring ways to align contingency and early transition planning for the handover of tasks related to children and armed conflict and will initiate a mapping of relevant actors who can take over responsibilities, resources and risks associated with this mandate. In addition, UNMISS has initiated the revision of its protection of civilians strategy to ensure flexibility and coordination of approaches. The Mission has also begun to develop a plan to assess the long-term impact of capacity-building activities implemented through the women’s leadership programme and is advancing the implementation of the multi-year strategic vision and mission plan to operationalize resources allocated to key and mandated activities to strengthen and support the decision-making process.

children.text: 79.	The Mission’s efficiency gains stem primarily from a reduction in the planned deployment of personnel. This was achieved by replacing a military aviation unit with a commercial provider capable of operating in hostile environments, as well as by eliminating a military engineering company. The Mission also implemented recommendations from the civilian staffing review, resulting in the abolishment of 17 posts and the nationalization of selected international staff and United Nations Volunteer positions. Additional savings were realized through lower information and communications technology (ICT) support service rates and reduced fuel consumption following the connection of several mission locations to the local power grid.

82.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali. In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.

84.	The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account the vacancy factors outlined in the table below. The table also summarizes, by category, the actual rates in the periods 2022/23 to 2024/25, as well as the approved and current rates for the 2025/26 period.

85.	The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishments. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12-month average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. A vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied when calculating the costs associated with proposed reassignments and conversions.
H.	Analysis of requirements for air operations

86.	The flight hour utilization rates for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, the approved and proposed number of flight hours for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods and the number of flight hours calculated on the basis of the policy guidance are as follows:

87.	The Mission will deploy 22 aircraft, compared with 25 deployed in 2025/26, to facilitate the movement of personnel and life support items across the mission area. In this regard, the Mission requires 16,054 flight hours, comprising 8,764 for fixed-wing aircraft and 7,290 for helicopters. The lower number of aircraft and flight hours reflects the replacement of five helicopters operated by a military aviation unit with two specialized commercial helicopters capable of operating in high‑risk environments. The replacement will generate efficiencies estimated at $4.7 million owing to the non-requirement of military personnel normally required for military aviation units and reduced fuel consumption resulting from fewer flight hours. The deployment takes into consideration the Mission’s unique operational context, including the prolonged rainy season and inadequate road infrastructure, which render air assets the only reliable mode of transportation to some locations in the mission area. The proposed number of flight hours is based on the Mission’s pattern of flight utilization rates and is in line with the policy guidance on aviation requirements.

The proposed resource requirements for the 2026/27 period will enable UNMISS to implement 80 quick-impact projects to further enhance confidence in the Mission, its mandate and the peace processes. These will focus on improving protection in areas affected by violence and displacement; supporting local authorities in facilitating return and reintegration through basic services; and fostering civic engagement in constitution-making and political processes, with an emphasis on women, young people and internally displaced persons.
