115.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the lower actual number of trips compared with the budgeted estimates owing to austerity measures established to manage the ongoing liquidity challenges.

116.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the non-deployment of additional military and police personnel for which a provision was included in the approved budget, resulting in: (i) the acquisition of fewer prefabricated facilities, accommodation and refrigeration equipment, engineering supplies, and water treatment and fuel distribution equipment; and (ii) the lower actual volume of 28.5 million litres acquired at the lower actual average price of $1.135 compared with the budgeted volume of 30.6 million litres at an average price of $1.145 per litre; and (b) the prioritization of resources to curtail operational costs in response to the ongoing liquidity challenges.
117.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to:

(a)	Lower costs for fuel for generators owing to: (i) the lower estimated volume of 20.7 million litres of fuel for generators at the lower average price of $1.096 per litre owing to the closure of field locations and the establishment of contractual arrangements to provide electricity from renewable sources to the Mission’s compounds, compared with the volume of 24.8 million litres at the higher average price of $1.156 included in the approved budget for the 20256/26 period; and (ii) the exclusion of non-recurrent provision for mobilization costs;

(b)	Reduced requirements for maintenance services, owing to: (i) lower camp maintenance costs compared with the cost included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period, due to the closure of several locations; and (ii) lower operation and maintenance costs for power generation and distribution systems resulting from efficiency gains achieved through the consolidation and synchronization of generators across the Mission.

118.	The reduced requirements are offset in part by higher costs for construction material and field defence supplies compared with estimates included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period, owing to the anticipated increased use of crushed aggregate in place of river gravel materials, reflecting a strategic shift intended to enhance structural stability and ensure greater material consistency.

119.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the non-deployment of additional military and police personnel for which a provision was included in the approved budget, resulting in a lower actual volume of 3.1 million litres acquired at a lower actual average price of $1.098 compared with the budgeted volume of 4.9 million litres at an average price of $1.116 per litre; and (b) the acquisition of fewer vehicles compared with the budgeted estimates, owing to the curtailment of operational costs in response to the ongoing liquidity challenges.

120.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the lower estimated volume of 2.7 million litres of fuel for vehicles at the lower average price of $1.065 per litre owing to cost-reduction measures and based on consumption trends, compared with the volume of 3.3 million litres at the average price of $1.116 included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period.
121.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to:

(a)	Lower costs for the rental and operation of fixed-wing aircraft owing to: (i) the non-utilization of the C-130 aircraft contracted for shared use across missions under a letter of assist, given the relatively high cost associated with its use; (ii) the lower number of flight hours compared with the budgeted estimates, resulting from the prolonged downtime owing to the unavailability of spare parts for some type of aircraft and the cancellation of flights due to adverse weather conditions during the rainy season; and (iii) the replacement of one aircraft with a more cost-efficient alternative;

(b)	The lower actual volume of 11.4 million litres acquired at a lower actual average price of $1.094 compared with the budgeted volume of 15.1 million litres at an average price of $1.128 per litre. The reduced requirements were offset in part by the net impact of the cost to deploy specialized helicopters to replace the Mi-17s suspended on safety grounds and savings realized from the non-utilization of the grounded Mi-17s.
122.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to:

(a)	The lower estimated volume of 11.0 million litres of fuel for aviation at a lower average price of $1.082 per litre compared with the volume of 12.9 million litres at an average price of $1.156 included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period;

(b)	The reduced requirements for the rental and operation of helicopters attributable to: (i) the exclusion of a non-recurring provision for repainting and positioning, which was included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period; and (ii) the lower rental and operation costs reflected in the new contracts for some aircraft.

123.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the acquisition of fewer shipping containers compared with the budgeted estimates owing to the non-deployment of additional military and police personnel; and (b) lower costs for the rental and operation of marine vessels owing to the suspension of barge services to allow sufficient time for the rehabilitation of the barge in accordance with new contractual specifications.

124.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable to the lower costs reflected in the new agreement for accommodation barge compared with the cost included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period.

125.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) higher costs for communications and information technology equipment owing to the replacement of the higher number of equipment deemed critical to the functioning of ICT systems compared with the budgeted estimates, which had exceeded their useful economical lifespan; and (b) higher costs for telecommunications and network services owing to the higher mission share of the cost of centralized services following the closure of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali. The increased requirements were offset in part by reduced requirements for the maintenance of communications and information technology equipment and support services owing to postponement of the engagement of ICT contractual personnel, as well as the delayed deployment of satellite earth stations at two field offices as part of the austerity measures established to manage liquidity challenges.

126.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the lower rates for centrally managed ICT services; and (b) a reduction in the number of ICT contractors, owing to discontinued operations in closed locations as part of cost-reduction measures.

127.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the acquisition of fewer medical supplies compared with the budgeted estimates, owing to the existence of adequate inventory acquired from previous years; and (b) lower costs for medical services for specialist consultations and the hospitalization of uniformed personnel at non-mission hospitals. The reduced requirements were offset in part by the acquisition equipment required for the level I clinics, for which a provision was not included in the approved budget.

128.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) lower freight costs, owing to: (i) the non-acquisition of engineering equipment and supplies, for which a provision was included in the approved budget, given the non-deployment of additional military and police personnel; and (ii) the substantial reduction in cargo tonnage movements within the mission area; and (b) lower costs for mine detection and mine-clearing services attributable to the prioritization of resources aimed at curtailing operational costs in response to the ongoing liquidity challenges.

129.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) lower freight costs owing to the planned acquisition of fewer equipment items and supplies from outside of the mission area compared with the estimates included in the approved budget for the 2025/26 period; and (b) a lower quantity of packaging material and other services owing to the reduced mission footprint.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 130.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Mission in South Sudan are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $38,504,900 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $12,319,800 from investment revenue ($5,510,400), other/miscellaneous revenue ($195,400) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($6,614,000);

(c)	To approve the apportionment of the amount of $50,824,700 among Member States in accordance with the applicable scale of assessment for 2025 and to place such apportioned amount in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates;

(d)	To allow Member States to apply their share of the amount apportioned in subparagraph (c) above towards current or future dues once the General Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has improved sufficiently to allow such application;
(e)	To appropriate the amount of $1,057,591,600 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027;
(f)	To assess the amount in subparagraph (e) above at a monthly rate of $88,132,633 should the Security Council decide to continue the mandate of the Mission.

text: V.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/309, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: A.	General Assembly
Cross-cutting issues
(Resolution 76/274)

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 31 December 2025, 5 of the 9 open recommendations (56 per cent) of the Board of Auditors pertaining to the 2023/24 period and 39 of the 56 recommendations (70 per cent) of OIOS had been implemented and closed.
Regular follow-up is also undertaken by UNMISS to ensure the timely closure of the remaining recommendations.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).

The mission support ratio (total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 90.9, slightly lower than the ratio of 91.7 in the 2020/21 period.
The Mission will continue to take mission support scalability into consideration in future budget reports.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

The proposed budget for the 2026/27 period includes the nationalization of two posts and four international United Nations Volunteer positions, in accordance with the recommendations of the civilian staffing review. Additional nationalizations recommended in the review will be implemented in a phased manner and reflected in subsequent budgets.

Recalls the provisions of section XVIII of its resolution 61/276, recognizes the important role played by quick-impact projects in establishing and building confidence in missions, further recognizes the importance of conducting assessments of the quick-impact projects regularly, on their needs and impact as required in resolution 61/276, requests the Secretary-General to include the conducted assessments in his next reports, and also requests the Secretary-General to enhance their impact (para. 82).

In line with the resolution, the Mission will regularly assess the needs and impact of quick-impact projects, and the results will continue to be reflected in the performance and budget reports of the Secretary-General on UNMISS. Efforts will also be made to enhance the effectiveness of quick-impact projects through improved planning, monitoring and community engagement.
An independent evaluation of quick-impact projects was conducted during the 2024/25 period, covering activities implemented from 2020/21 to 2024/25. A total of 283 projects were carried out across several areas.

The evaluation found that the projects contributed to building trust, acceptance and credibility for UNMISS by delivering tangible improvements in security and in access to water, health, education and humanitarian assistance. The projects also enhanced community engagement, strengthened social cohesion and supported the peace process, contributing to voluntary returns, reintegration and increased confidence in law enforcement and public institutions.
It was cautioned, however, that sustaining those gains and achieving lasting peace would require addressing intercommunal conflict, political instability and the implementation of the remaining peace benchmarks.

children.text: B.	Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Financing of the of the United Nations Mission in South Sudan
(A/79/724/Add.12 and General Assembly resolution 79/309)

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee recalls General Assembly resolution 76/274 and considers that, as a matter of principle, the Secretary-General should ensure the continuous optimization of the staffing structures, including through civilian and security staffing reviews, with the aim to identify efficiencies and trusts that UNMISS will conduct a civilian staffing review as soon as possible (para. 29).
A civilian staffing review was conducted during the 2025/26 period, as described in paragraph 51 of the present report.

The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the status of vacant posts and positions, as well as on the impact of the liquidity situation on projected recruitments, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission (para. 30).

The number of vacant posts and positions is taken into account when determining the proposed vacancy rates. Information on long-vacant posts and positions is provided in the supplementary information, while the updated status of all vacant posts and positions is provided during the legislative review of the performance and budget report.

The Advisory Committee trusts that UNMISS will ensure that projects benefit the local population, as intended, and that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next proposed budget report (A/79/724/Add.1, para. 28) (para. 43).

Information on quick-impact projects implemented during the 2024/25 period is provided in paragraph 23 of the present report. 
The Mission works with community development grant committees composed of community leaders, women’s representatives, youth leaders, local civil society organizations and local authorities to ensure that funded projects address the needs of communities and deliver direct benefits to local populations. This participatory process also promotes local ownership and enhances the sustainability of projects, helping to address protection concerns, fill service gaps and support communities in realizing early peace dividends.

The Advisory Committee notes the efficiency gains identified by UNMISS for the 2025/26 period and trusts that further efficiency gains will be explored and reflected in future budget proposals (para. 45).
The Advisory Committee acknowledges the efforts of the Mission to implement renewable energy projects and trusts that further efficiency gains will be generated from energy initiatives and will be reflected in future reports (para. 46).

The Mission continues to advance its efforts to expand the use of renewable energy. Efficiency gains resulting from these renewable energy initiatives are estimated at $0.98 million for the 2026/27 period.
The Advisory Committee trusts that UNMISS will further pursue its efforts to promote equitable geographical representation and gender parity in the Mission (para. 47).

UNMISS maintains a diverse workforce, with international civilian personnel from 117 countries and United Nations Volunteers from 82 countries as at 31 December 2025. As at the same date, the Mission had 852 international staff drawn from the following regional groups: Africa (446), Asia-Pacific (179), Eastern Europe (97), Latin America and the Caribbean (27) and Western Europe and other (103).
To advance gender parity and broaden geographical representation, UNMISS has issued position-specific job openings to attract a wider applicant pool, incorporated standard language in job openings affirming the Mission’s commitment to 50/50 gender parity and raised the awareness of hiring managers through the regular dissemination of gender and geographical data to inform selection processes.
